canonical_summary:
Nuclear compliance is the discipline of meeting the body of binding laws, regulator-issued rules, license conditions, technical codes, and international safety standards that govern civilian nuclear activity — primarily reactor design, construction, operation, fuel handling, security, export, and decommissioning. In the United States it centers on the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974, and Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations (Parts 50, 52, 53, 73, 100, 110), administered by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC). Internationally it is shaped by the IAEA Safety Standards Series — anchored by SF-1 (Fundamental Safety Principles) and SSR-2/1 (Safety of Nuclear Power Plants: Design) — and implemented through national regulators including the UK Office for Nuclear Regulation, Canada's CNSC, Japan's NRA, France's ASNR, Korea's NSSC, and India's AERB. The work product is dominated by Safety Analysis Reports (PSAR at construction-permit stage, FSAR at operating-license stage), Environmental Reports, Technical Specifications, Quality Assurance Program Descriptions, Emergency Plans, and the iterative cycle of Requests for Additional Information (RAIs) that drive review schedules.

For advanced reactor developers in 2026, the compliance landscape has been transformed by the NRC's final 10 CFR Part 53 rule (published 30 March 2026), a risk-informed, technology-inclusive optional framework created under NEIMA (2019) and reinforced by the ADVANCE Act (2024), alongside the parallel microreactor rule (1 May 2026), reduced applicant hourly fees, and recent throughput wins (NuScale US460 SDA in 22 months; Kairos Hermes 2 CP in 10 months; Natrium FSER 9 months early). The persistent pain points — LWR-shaped legacy regulations, immature non-LWR PRA methodologies, novel-fuel source-term defenses, document volumes routinely exceeding 15,000 pages, citation-grade traceability across thousands of pages of guidance, and RAI cycles that can run into the hundreds — are the operating reality of the discipline. Invariant's Helion-512 retrieval model achieves a published 0.97 nDCG@10 on FermiBench, the strongest evidence to date that domain-specific retrieval can support the citation-accurate, regulation-grounded work that nuclear compliance demands.
h1: Nuclear Compliance: Regulators, Rules, Licensing Pathways, and Safety Analysis Reports
meta_title: Nuclear Compliance: Regulators, 10 CFR, PSAR/FSAR | Invariant
meta_description: A primary-source map of nuclear compliance: NRC, IAEA, DOE, ONR, CNSC; 10 CFR Parts 50/52/53/100/110; PSAR/FSAR drafting; advanced reactor and SMR licensing in 2026.

sections.heading: What Nuclear Compliance Means
paragraphs: Nuclear compliance is the discipline of meeting the body of binding laws, regulator-issued rules, license conditions, technical codes, and international safety standards that govern civilian nuclear activity. In practice it covers reactor siting, design, construction, fuel fabrication and handling, operation, security, radioactive waste, decommissioning, and export of nuclear equipment, material, and technology. It is a documentation-intensive, citation-grade discipline: every safety claim must be traceable to a regulatory basis, a code or standard, and an analytical or experimental justification.

The field sits at the intersection of three legal layers. The statutory layer in the United States is anchored by the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 and the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974, which together created an independent civilian regulator (the NRC) and a separate sponsor and steward (DOE). The regulatory layer is Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations, supplemented by NRC Regulatory Guides, NUREG documents, Standard Review Plans, and topical reports. The international layer is the IAEA Safety Standards Series — non-binding but substantively adopted by every serious national regulator — together with the bilateral 123 agreements that govern export and safeguards.

For commercial developers — particularly the advanced reactor, small modular reactor (SMR), and microreactor cohort active in 2026 — nuclear compliance is the gating activity for capital deployment. A defensible compliance posture determines whether a design can be licensed, where it can be sited, how quickly it can reach commercial operation, and whether it can be exported. The work product is dominated by Safety Analysis Reports (PSAR and FSAR), Environmental Reports, Technical Specifications, Quality Assurance Program Descriptions, Emergency Plans, security plans under 10 CFR Part 73, and the iterative cycle of Requests for Additional Information (RAIs) that drive review schedules.

sections.heading: Major Regulators and Their Roles
paragraphs: Nuclear compliance is administered by national regulators acting under domestic statute, coordinated through the IAEA and bilateral treaty obligations. The same physics is regulated differently in each jurisdiction, and a developer pursuing multi-country deployment must satisfy each regime independently.

subsections.heading: U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)
paragraphs: The NRC is the independent civilian regulator created by the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974. Under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954 it licenses all civilian commercial reactors, research and test reactors, fuel cycle facilities, byproduct and source material users, and nuclear exports. It promulgates regulations in 10 CFR, issues Regulatory Guides documenting acceptable means of compliance, and runs site-specific and topical reviews staffed by technical reviewers and the Advisory Committee on Reactor Safeguards (ACRS). It also delegates byproduct, source, and small-quantity special nuclear material regulation to Agreement States under Section 274 of the Atomic Energy Act.

subsections.heading: U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)
paragraphs: DOE owns and operates the government nuclear complex — including national-laboratory test reactors, the demonstration reactors at Idaho National Laboratory, and the defense complex — and funds the Advanced Reactor Demonstration Program. DOE also administers technology-and-assistance export controls under 10 CFR Part 810, distinct from NRC's equipment-and-material export authority under 10 CFR Part 110. For commercial developers, DOE is both a customer (cost share, ARDP), a landlord (INL test beds), and a parallel export authority.

subsections.heading: International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)
paragraphs: The IAEA issues the Safety Standards Series — Fundamentals, Requirements, and Guides — which are non-binding but are substantively adopted by most national regulators. The two apex documents are SF-1 (Fundamental Safety Principles) and the Specific Safety Requirements, of which SSR-2/1 (Safety of Nuclear Power Plants: Design) and SSR-2/2 (Commissioning and Operation) are the most-cited for civilian reactors. The IAEA also administers safeguards inspections under NPT Article III and related agreements.

subsections.heading: UK Office for Nuclear Regulation (ONR)
paragraphs: The ONR runs the Generic Design Assessment (GDA) jointly with the Environment Agency and Natural Resources Wales, then licenses sites under the Nuclear Installations Act 1965. The GDA is a structured pre-licensing review intended to resolve generic design issues before site-specific licensing. Rolls-Royce SMR, GE-Hitachi BWRX-300, and Holtec SMR-300 are currently in GDA at various steps as of 2026.

subsections.heading: Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission (CNSC)
paragraphs: The CNSC licenses under the Nuclear Safety and Control Act and offers an optional pre-licensing Vendor Design Review (VDR) in three phases under REGDOC-3.5.4 (formerly GD-385). The VDR is not a license but identifies fundamental barriers; Phase 2 has been completed for Terrestrial IMSR, GEH BWRX-300, and X-energy Xe-100, among others. The CNSC is widely regarded as the most experienced SMR pre-licensing reviewer.

subsections.heading: Other National Regulators
paragraphs: Japan's Nuclear Regulation Authority (NRA), reconstituted in 2012 after Fukushima, issues establishment permits and runs conformity reviews against the New Regulatory Requirements. France's Autorité de Sûreté Nucléaire et de Radioprotection (ASNR), formed on 1 January 2025 by merging ASN and the technical support body IRSN, runs a four-phase incremental review for innovative reactor and SMR projects. India's Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) applies a stage-wise consenting process — siting, construction, commissioning, operation — under the Atomic Energy Act 1962. South Korea's Nuclear Safety and Security Commission (NSSC), supported by the Korea Institute of Nuclear Safety (KINS), is preparing the legislative framework for SMRs including the domestic i-SMR.

sections.heading: Major U.S. Regulations: 10 CFR Parts 50, 52, 53, 100, and 110
paragraphs: Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations is the operative rulebook for U.S. civilian nuclear activity. Five parts dominate practice for commercial reactor developers.

subsections.heading: 10 CFR Part 50 — Domestic Licensing of Production and Utilization Facilities
paragraphs: Part 50 establishes the classic two-step licensing pathway: a Construction Permit (CP) followed by an Operating License (OL). The CP application requires a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) and an Environmental Report; the OL application requires a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) describing the as-built final design, identifying changes from the PSAR, and discussing the bases for safety. Part 50 is the path under which the existing U.S. fleet was licensed and is still used for first-of-a-kind units where the design must iterate up to OL submission — including Kairos Power's Hermes demonstration reactor and TerraPower's Natrium Kemmerer Unit 1.

subsections.heading: 10 CFR Part 52 — Licenses, Certifications, and Approvals
paragraphs: Part 52 introduces a one-stop pathway centered on design standardization. Its three principal instruments are the Early Site Permit (ESP) under Subpart A, which banks site characteristics for up to 20 years; the Standard Design Certification (DC) under Subpart B, in which the Commission approves a final standard design and codifies it as an Appendix to Part 52 (issued DCs include AP1000, ESBWR, APR1400, and NuScale US600); and the Combined License (COL) under Subpart C, which merges construction permit and conditioned operating license. Subpart F additionally provides for a Manufacturing License covering factory-built reactors shipped to a separately licensed site. The NuScale US460 received a Standard Design Approval — a lower-cost intermediate to a full DC — in 22 months in 2025.

subsections.heading: 10 CFR Part 53 — Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Framework
paragraphs: Mandated by the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (NEIMA, 2019) and reinforced by the ADVANCE Act of 2024, the final 10 CFR Part 53 rule was published in the Federal Register on 30 March 2026. Part 53 is an optional alternative to Parts 50 and 52 for commercial advanced reactors. Headline provisions include Section 53.450 (risk methodologies — explicit allowance of PRA, other systematic risk evaluations, or combinations, with no prescriptive method imposed), Section 53.440 (codes-and-standards latitude with graded use and non-nuclear codes permitted for low-safety-significance SSCs), and Section 53.620 (factory fueling of manufactured reactors before shipment). A new performance-based physical security framework was added to 10 CFR Part 73 in parallel. A companion microreactor rule, Licensing Requirements for Microreactors and Other Reactors With Comparable Risk Profiles, was published 1 May 2026.

subsections.heading: 10 CFR Part 100 — Reactor Site Criteria
paragraphs: Part 100 defines three concentric zones around a reactor site: an exclusion area where the licensee controls access and residence is prohibited, a Low Population Zone (LPZ) where protective action must be feasible, and a population center distance that must be at least one and one-third times the distance to the LPZ outer boundary. Post-design-basis-accident dose criteria are 25 rem whole body and 300 rem thyroid (iodine) at the exclusion-area boundary over two hours and during cloud passage at the LPZ. Subpart A retains evaluation factors for pre-1997 applicants and test reactors; Subpart B consolidates modern siting with cross-references to Part 50 Appendix A. The Emergency Planning Zone rulemaking finalized in 2023 (RIN 3150-AK11) was the necessary unlock to permit small and co-located EPZs for SMRs and microreactors.

subsections.heading: 10 CFR Part 110 — Export and Import of Nuclear Equipment and Material
paragraphs: Part 110 establishes NRC's export licensing authority for nuclear facility equipment — especially designed or prepared (ED/P) components — special nuclear material (plutonium, highly enriched uranium, uranium-233), source material, and byproduct material. Imports are also covered. Part 110 licenses carry obligations flowing from 123 agreements — peaceful end-use assurances, prior consent for retransfer, and IAEA safeguards conditions. DOE's parallel Part 810 authorization regime governs export of nuclear technology and assistance, not equipment, and the two regimes apply serially to commercial advanced-reactor exports.

sections.heading: IAEA Safety Standards: SF-1 and SSR-2/1
paragraphs: The IAEA Safety Standards Series is the international substrate beneath every credible national regulatory regime. Two documents anchor it for civilian reactors.

subsections.heading: SF-1 — Fundamental Safety Principles (2006)
paragraphs: SF-1 establishes a single fundamental safety objective — protect people and the environment from harmful effects of ionizing radiation — and ten principles: (1) responsibility for safety lies with the operator, (2) role of government and an independent regulator, (3) leadership and management for safety, (4) justification of facilities and activities, (5) optimization of protection (ALARA), (6) limitation of risks to individuals, (7) protection of present and future generations, (8) prevention of accidents, (9) emergency preparedness and response, and (10) protective action to reduce existing or unregulated radiation risks. SF-1 applies across all facility lifetimes and is the apex document under which the Specific Safety Requirements (SSRs) and Specific Safety Guides (SSGs) sit.

subsections.heading: SSR-2/1 (Rev. 1) — Safety of Nuclear Power Plants: Design (2016)
paragraphs: SSR-2/1 replaced NS-R-1; Revision 1 incorporates Fukushima lessons. It establishes design requirements for nuclear power plants, including defense-in-depth, plant states (including design extension conditions), the single failure criterion, the fundamental safety functions (reactivity control, decay-heat removal, confinement of radioactive material), safety classification of structures, systems, and components, and protection against internal and external hazards. The companion SSR-2/2 (Rev. 1) covers commissioning and operation. SSR-2/1 is used wholesale by ONR, CNSC, ASNR, NRA, AERB, and NSSC as the reference safety baseline for SMR and advanced reactor reviews; the NRC aligns informally rather than by adoption.

sections.heading: Licensing Pathways for Advanced Reactors, SMRs, and Microreactors in 2026
paragraphs: Developers active in 2026 face a menu of licensing pathways. The right choice depends on design maturity, deployment model, and customer mix.

Part 50 (CP then OL) suits a design that is still maturing, a first-of-a-kind demonstration, or any program that needs to pour concrete on a flexible design. Kairos's Hermes reactors and TerraPower's Natrium use this path. The price is two separate hearings and two separate NEPA passes.

Part 52 with a Design Certification plus a Combined License suits mature, standardized designs intended to be built at multiple sites — the AP1000 and VOYGR (US600) playbook. The DC carries high up-front cost but creates design finality; later changes require rulemaking-grade amendments. The Standard Design Approval (used for NuScale US460) is a lower-cost intermediate that can be referenced in a CP or COL without the rulemaking step.
Part 52 Early Site Permits suit developers who want to bank a site before choosing a reactor (Vogtle, Clinch River). They resolve siting characteristics but not design.

The Part 52 Manufacturing License under Subpart F suits factory-built units pre-assembled and shipped to separately licensed sites — the Oklo and microreactor model. This pathway was historically underused; Part 53 Section 53.620 now expressly permits factory fueling of manufactured reactors.

Part 53 (effective 2026) is the new optional path for non-LWR designs — HTGR, MSR, SFR, LFR, microreactors — that do not fit the LWR-shaped boxes of Parts 50 and 52. Its risk-informed, performance-based architecture is the structural fix the advanced reactor cohort has waited a decade for; the immediate cost is that staff and applicant tooling are still maturing and no precedent Safety Evaluation Reports exist yet.

International analogues compress some of this work. The UK GDA, the CNSC Vendor Design Review, the ASNR four-phase incremental exchange, and the Korean NSSC/KINS pre-design reviews all let vendors resolve generic design issues before site-specific licensing. Developers pursuing multi-country deployment typically run several pre-licensing dialogues in parallel.

sections.heading: Safety Analysis Reports: PSAR and FSAR
paragraphs: The Safety Analysis Report is the central work product of nuclear compliance. Its preliminary form (PSAR) supports a Construction Permit under Part 50 or its Part 53 equivalent; its final form (FSAR) supports an Operating License or Combined License and is maintained as the Updated FSAR for the life of the plant under 10 CFR 50.71(e).

The authoritative drafting guidance is Regulatory Guide 1.70 (Rev. 3, November 1978), Standard Format and Content of Safety Analysis Reports for Nuclear Power Plants (LWR Edition). Although it has not been updated since 1978, its 19 chapters — Introduction, Site Characteristics, Design of Structures and Systems, Reactor, Reactor Coolant System, Engineered Safety Features, Instrumentation and Controls, Electric Power, Auxiliary Systems, Steam and Power Conversion, Radioactive Waste Management, Radiation Protection, Conduct of Operations, Initial Test Program, Accident Analysis, Technical Specifications, Quality Assurance, Review and Audit, and Special Topics — remain the structural backbone of every modern application. Regulatory Guide 1.206 updates this structure for COL applications and aligns it with the Standard Review Plan, NUREG-0800, which gives NRC staff section-by-section acceptance criteria. For advanced (non-LWR) reactors, the Licensing Modernization Project methodology in NEI 18-04 — endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.233 — provides a risk-informed, performance-based approach to selecting Licensing Basis Events and classifying structures, systems, and components. Part 53 builds on this.

The PSAR contains the preliminary design, site evaluation, design bases for SSCs, preliminary accident analyses with bounding assumptions, and preliminary Technical Specifications. Its purpose is to persuade the NRC that a plant satisfying the stated design bases can be built and operated safely on the chosen site. The FSAR resolves all PSAR open items and adds the as-built final design, the final accident analyses (design-basis accidents and beyond-design-basis sequences including ATWS and station blackout), the Technical Specifications, the Quality Assurance Program Description, the Emergency Plan, the Fire Protection Plan, and a separately documented Security Plan under Part 73.

The drafting reality is shaped by three factors. First is volume: a large LWR COL routinely runs 15,000 to 30,000 pages across the FSAR, Environmental Report, Technical Specifications, and supporting QA, EP, and security documentation; NuScale's Design Certification application ran roughly 14,000 pages of safety analysis alone. Second is the Chapter 15 accident analysis (for LWRs) or its risk-informed equivalent (for non-LWRs) — selecting Licensing Basis Events, defining the design-basis envelope, and demonstrating that source-term and dose consequences satisfy Part 100 and Part 50 Appendix I limits is where most engineering effort lands. Third is open-item and RAI management: the NRC issues Requests for Additional Information by the hundreds; each RAI response is reviewed, can spawn follow-ups, and must be reconciled into the SAR revision. Vendors routinely decouple methodology issues — fuel performance models, PRA methods, severe-accident codes — into separately reviewed topical reports that can be referenced from multiple SARs, the dominant cost-amortization tactic for FOAK developers.

Common RAI patterns include challenges to source-term derivation for novel fuels (TRISO, metallic, molten salt), questions on PRA scope and convergence for non-LWR designs, requests for additional justification of code-and-standard selection under graded approaches, demands for more rigorous treatment of beyond-design-basis sequences, and clarifications on the boundary between the licensed design and supporting topical reports.

sections.heading: Common Pain Points for Advanced Reactor Developers
paragraphs: Practitioners and the NRC itself have documented a consistent set of friction points across the past decade of advanced-reactor activity. The most important are structural rather than tactical.
bullets: Legacy LWR-shaped regulations. Parts 50 and 52, Regulatory Guide 1.70, and NUREG-0800 were written around large light-water plants. Non-LWRs (sodium, salt, gas, heat-pipe) have to argue by exception. Part 53 is the structural fix but is brand new with no precedent reviews yet.

PRA scope and maturity at preliminary design. Non-LWR PRAs are not standardized; the ASME/ANS PRA Standard is LWR-centric. LMP and NEI 18-04 help, but defining the frequency-consequence target curve and selecting Licensing Basis Events remain methodological debates.
Source term and dose assessment for novel fuels. Standard alternative source terms in NUREG-1465 do not apply to TRISO, metallic, or molten-salt fuels; vendors must derive and defend their own.

Site criteria mismatch. The Part 100 exclusion area and LPZ math was designed around 25-rem PWR LOCA source terms. For microreactors the technically defensible exclusion-area boundary shrinks to meters, requiring the EPZ rulemaking finalized in 2023 to unlock small and co-located EPZs.
Hearing and NEPA timelines. ACRS review, mandatory hearings, and Environmental Impact Statements remain schedule-dominant. ESPs can parallelize siting but do not resolve design.
NRC staffing. GAO-23-105997 (July 2023) documented persistent NRC difficulty hiring and retaining staff with non-LWR expertise and a lack of benchmarks to evaluate recruitment strategies.

Cost recovery and hourly rates. Until FY25 the NRC charged the full professional hourly rate (~$318/hr). Under the ADVANCE Act the advanced-reactor applicant and pre-applicant rate has dropped to $148/hr — a meaningful change for capital-constrained developers.

Pre-application engagement is essentially mandatory in practice. Kairos Hermes 1 and 2, TerraPower Kemmerer, and NuScale US460 all involved years of structured pre-application meetings. Oklo's 2020 Aurora application — denied without prejudice — is widely cited as the cautionary tale of skipping deep pre-application engagement.

Citation accuracy and document depth. Cross-references span tens of thousands of pages of guidance and code; a single broken citation, outdated regulatory reference, or misaligned Regulatory Guide vintage can cascade into RAIs.

Regulation evolution. Part 53, the microreactor rule, the EPZ rule, ADVANCE Act-driven fee changes, and ongoing security and emergency planning rulemakings mean the regulatory baseline itself moves during a multi-year licensing program.

Export friction. Even after a domestic license, exporting a unit requires NRC Part 110 authorization plus DOE Part 810 technology authorization plus the destination country's 123 agreement obligations — a serial, multi-agency chokepoint.

Recent throughput wins are real but uneven: NuScale US460 SDA in 22 months (2 months ahead of schedule, 13% under budget), Kairos Hermes 2 CP in 10 months (60% resource reduction versus Hermes 1), and the Natrium Kemmerer Unit 1 FSER 9 months early and 11% under budget. These suggest the system can move fast on well-prepared, well-engaged applications.

sections.heading: How Autonomous AI Agents Are Changing Nuclear Compliance Work
paragraphs: Nuclear compliance is a domain where every load-bearing claim must be traceable to a primary source. Until recently this made it a poor fit for general-purpose AI tools: hallucinated citations, stale regulatory text, and incorrect cross-references between Part 50, Part 52, NUREG-0800, and the IAEA Safety Standards Series are not tolerable errors in a PSAR or an RAI response. The shift over the past 18 months has been the emergence of domain-specific retrieval and reasoning systems that can operate at citation-grade fidelity over the full corpus of NRC regulations, Regulatory Guides, NUREGs, SECY papers, ACRS letters, and IAEA safety standards.

Invariant's position is straightforward: autonomous agents do not replace nuclear engineers, regulatory specialists, or legal counsel. They compress the load-bearing but mechanically tractable parts of the work — locating the right Regulatory Guide vintage, cross-walking a non-LWR design feature to the Part 53 framework, drafting a defensible RAI response with traceable citations, identifying inconsistencies between SAR chapters, and surfacing precedent SERs and topical-report references. The result is review-ready drafts produced in hours rather than weeks, with every claim auditable back to a primary source.

Invariant's Helion-512 retrieval model achieves a published 0.97 nDCG@10 on FermiBench, our domain-specific evaluation set for technical and regulatory retrieval. To our knowledge this is the state of the art on FermiBench and is the strongest evidence to date that AI systems can operate at the citation density and accuracy that nuclear compliance demands. We treat this benchmark as a floor, not a ceiling: the work product that matters is whether a generated PSAR chapter, RAI response, or topical report stands up to a careful reviewer's scrutiny, and Helion-512's retrieval fidelity is what makes that possible.

sections.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
paragraphs: Common questions from developers, investors, and operating staff approaching nuclear compliance for the first time.

faqs.question: What is nuclear compliance?
faqs.answer: Nuclear compliance is the discipline of meeting the binding laws, regulations, license conditions, technical codes, and international safety standards that govern civilian nuclear activity — including reactor design, construction, operation, fuel handling, security, export, and decommissioning. In the United States it centers on the Atomic Energy Act of 1954, Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations, and NRC oversight; internationally it is shaped by the IAEA Safety Standards Series.

faqs.question: Who regulates nuclear power plants in the United States?
faqs.answer: The U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC), an independent agency created by the Energy Reorganization Act of 1974, licenses and regulates all civilian commercial reactors, fuel cycle facilities, and nuclear exports under the Atomic Energy Act of 1954. The Department of Energy owns and operates the government nuclear complex and administers separate technology-export controls under 10 CFR Part 810.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 10 CFR Part 50 and Part 52?
faqs.answer: Part 50 is the classic two-step pathway: Construction Permit (with a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report) followed by an Operating License (with a Final Safety Analysis Report). Part 52 is a one-stop pathway built around design standardization, with Early Site Permits, Standard Design Certifications, and Combined Licenses. Part 50 is preferred when the design is still maturing; Part 52 is preferred for mature, standardized designs intended for multiple sites.

faqs.question: What is 10 CFR Part 53?
faqs.answer: Part 53 is the NRC's risk-informed, technology-inclusive optional licensing framework for commercial advanced reactors. Mandated by NEIMA (2019) and reinforced by the ADVANCE Act (2024), the final rule was published in the Federal Register on 30 March 2026. It allows PRA-driven safety cases, graded use of consensus codes, factory fueling of manufactured reactors, and a performance-based physical security framework — designed specifically for non-LWR designs that do not fit Parts 50 and 52.

faqs.question: What does the IAEA do in nuclear compliance?
faqs.answer: The IAEA issues the Safety Standards Series — Safety Fundamentals (SF-1), Specific Safety Requirements (such as SSR-2/1 for NPP design), and Specific Safety Guides — which are non-binding but adopted in substance by most national regulators. The IAEA also administers safeguards inspections under NPT Article III and supports member states with peer reviews and missions.

faqs.question: What is a PSAR?
faqs.answer: A Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) is the central technical document supporting a Construction Permit application under 10 CFR Part 50 or its Part 53 equivalent. It contains the preliminary design, site evaluation, design bases for structures, systems, and components, preliminary accident analyses with bounding assumptions, and preliminary Technical Specifications, structured according to Regulatory Guide 1.70.

faqs.question: What is an FSAR?
faqs.answer: A Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) supports an Operating License or Combined License application. It documents the as-built final design, resolves all PSAR open items, contains final accident analyses (design-basis and beyond-design-basis), Technical Specifications, the Quality Assurance Program Description, the Emergency Plan, and the Fire Protection Plan. Under 10 CFR 50.71(e) the FSAR must be kept current as the Updated FSAR for the life of the operating license.

faqs.question: How long does NRC reactor licensing take?
faqs.answer: Schedules vary by pathway and design maturity. Recent throughput wins include NuScale's US460 Standard Design Approval in 22 months (2 months ahead of schedule, 13% under budget), Kairos's Hermes 2 Construction Permit in 10 months (about 60% less resource than Hermes 1), and TerraPower Natrium's Kemmerer Unit 1 Final Safety Evaluation Report 9 months early and 11% under budget. Combined License timelines for large LWRs historically ran 3 to 4 years post-submission.

faqs.question: What is an RAI?
faqs.answer: A Request for Additional Information (RAI) is a formal NRC staff request for clarification, additional analysis, or supplementary documentation during the review of a license application or amendment. RAIs are issued by the hundreds for a major application, must be tracked and reconciled into the SAR revision, and frequently spawn follow-up RAIs. RAI cycle management is one of the dominant cost and schedule drivers of nuclear licensing.

faqs.question: What is the ADVANCE Act?
faqs.answer: The Accelerating Deployment of Versatile, Advanced Nuclear for Clean Energy (ADVANCE) Act, signed into law in July 2024, directs the NRC to update its mission to consider the benefits of nuclear deployment, reduce licensing fees for advanced reactor applicants and pre-applicants (the hourly rate dropped from approximately $318/hr to $148/hr), streamline environmental reviews, and authorize new awards and prizes for first-of-a-kind advanced reactor licensing.

faqs.question: How are SMRs and microreactors licensed?
faqs.answer: Small modular reactors and microreactors can be licensed under Part 50, Part 52, or — as of 30 March 2026 — Part 53. The Part 52 Manufacturing License under Subpart F is well suited to factory-built units, and Part 53 Section 53.620 expressly permits factory fueling. A companion microreactor rule, Licensing Requirements for Microreactors and Other Reactors With Comparable Risk Profiles, was published in the Federal Register on 1 May 2026.

faqs.question: What is the role of the IAEA's SSR-2/1?
faqs.answer: SSR-2/1 (Rev. 1, 2016), Safety of Nuclear Power Plants: Design, establishes Specific Safety Requirements for NPP design — defense-in-depth, plant states including design extension conditions, the single failure criterion, fundamental safety functions (reactivity control, decay-heat removal, confinement), safety classification of SSCs, and protection against internal and external hazards. It is used as the reference safety baseline by ONR, CNSC, ASNR, NRA, AERB, and NSSC.

faqs.question: Do advanced reactor developers need to engage with the NRC before applying?
faqs.answer: Yes, in practical terms. Every recent advanced-reactor success — Kairos Hermes 1 and 2, TerraPower Kemmerer, NuScale US460 — involved years of structured pre-application meetings with NRC staff. The 2020 Oklo Aurora application, denied without prejudice for completeness and methodology reasons, is widely cited as the cautionary tale of insufficient pre-application engagement.

faqs.question: How are nuclear exports regulated?
faqs.answer: Commercial nuclear exports are regulated under a serial multi-agency regime: the NRC issues licenses for nuclear facility equipment and material under 10 CFR Part 110; the Department of Energy authorizes export of nuclear technology and assistance under 10 CFR Part 810; and the destination country must be a party to a peaceful-use cooperation agreement under Section 123 of the Atomic Energy Act. IAEA safeguards conditions and prior consent for retransfer attach to each export.

faqs.question: How can AI agents help with nuclear compliance work?
faqs.answer: Domain-specific retrieval and reasoning systems can compress the mechanically tractable parts of compliance work — locating the right Regulatory Guide vintage, cross-walking design features to Part 53, drafting RAI responses with traceable citations, and identifying inconsistencies between SAR chapters. Invariant's Helion-512 retrieval model achieves a published 0.97 nDCG@10 on FermiBench, the state of the art on that benchmark and our strongest evidence that AI systems can operate at the citation density nuclear compliance demands.
